questions.question: Define the validity of a well-formed formula (wff)?
questions.answer: A wff is valid (a tautology) if and only if it evaluates to True under every possible assignment of truth values to its propositional variables.
questions.to_find: The precise definition of when a well-formed formula is valid.

understand.plain: A well-formed formula is built from propositional variables and connectives following the grammar rules of propositional logic. Such a formula is 'valid' when it can never come out false: no matter what True/False values you plug into its variables, the whole formula stays True. Validity is the strongest kind of always-true; a valid wff is exactly a tautology.

keywords.explain: a syntactically legal logic expression made from variables and connectives (not, and, or, ->, <->)
keywords.example: (p -> q) is a wff; '-> p q ) and' is not

keywords.explain: a choice of True or False for every propositional variable in the formula
keywords.example: for p, q one assignment is p = True, q = False

keywords.explain: true for every truth assignment; the last column of its truth table is all True
keywords.example: p or (not p) is valid because it is True whether p is True or False

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Validity classification by truth table'><title>Validity of a wff</title><desc>A wff is valid when its truth table column is all True, satisfiable when at least one row is True, and unsatisfiable when every row is False.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe class='lbl ink'>all rows</text><text x='105' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='big ok'>T</text><text x='105' y='162' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>tautology</text><rect x='205' y='50' class='bg'/><text x='455' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>UNSATISFIABLE</text><text x='455' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>all rows</text><text x='455' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='big no'>F</text><text x='455' y='162' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>contradiction</text><text x='280' y='212' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>valid = True in every interpretation</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A wff is valid (a tautology) when every row of its truth table is True; satisfiable means at least one True row, and unsatisfiable means every row is False.

given.aim: State exactly when a well-formed formula counts as valid.

terms.meaning: a syntactically correct propositional formula over some variables
terms.example: F = (p -> q) -> (not q -> not p)
terms.connects: validity is a property tested across all assignments to its variables

terms.meaning: an assignment of True/False to each variable of F
terms.example: I: p = True, q = False
terms.connects: F is evaluated to True or False under each I

terms.meaning: the value of F computed from I using the connective rules is True
terms.example: the example F above is True under every I
terms.connects: validity requires this to hold for all I, not just one

given.plan: Recall the semantic definition: quantify over all interpretations and require the formula to be True in each, which is the same as the truth-table column being uniformly True.

steps.title: Fix the meaning of an interpretation
steps.formula_raw: I : Var(F) -> {True, False}
steps.apply: An interpretation I assigns a truth value to every propositional variable that appears in F. With n variables there are 2^n distinct interpretations.
steps.note: This is exactly one row of the truth table of F.

steps.title: State the validity condition
steps.formula_raw: F is valid  iff  for all interpretations I, value(F, I) = True
steps.apply: F is valid (a tautology) precisely when value(F, I) = True for every interpretation I. Equivalently, the final column of the truth table of F contains only True.
steps.note: Contrast: satisfiable = True for at least one I; unsatisfiable (contradiction) = True for no I.

solution.result: A well-formed formula F is valid (a tautology) if and only if it is True under every interpretation, i.e. the last column of its truth table is all True.

questions.formula_note: The key idea is the semantic definition of validity: 'True under every interpretation', which equates validity with being a tautology. The official key marks this as N/A (descriptive).

questions.question: Which of the following well-formed formulas are equivalent? A. P -> Q   B. not Q -> not P   C. not P or Q   D. not Q -> P
questions.to_find: Which of the four formulas denote the same truth function (are logically equivalent).

understand.plain: Two formulas are equivalent when they have identical truth tables. The implication P -> Q can be rewritten in two famous ways: as its contrapositive not Q -> not P, and as the disjunction not P or Q (the material-implication law). All three are the same. The fourth, not Q -> P, simplifies to Q or P, which differs from P -> Q (they disagree when P and Q are both False).

keywords.explain: two formulas with the same truth value under every assignment; written X == Y
keywords.example: P -> Q == not P or Q

keywords.explain: swapping and negating both sides of an implication; always equivalent to the original
keywords.example: P -> Q has contrapositive not Q -> not P

keywords.explain: the rewrite of -> using or and not: A -> B == not A or B
keywords.example: P -> Q == not P or Q

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Truth tables comparison'><title>Equivalence of P->Q and friends</title><desc>P->Q, not Q->not P and not P or Q share the same truth column F-row only when P true Q false; not Q->P differs.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>P Q</text><text x='120' y='30' class='lbl ink'>A,B,C: P->Q</text><text x='370' y='30' class='lbl ink'>D: not Q->P</text><line x1='15' y1='40' x2='540' y2='40' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='20' y='70' class='sub ink'>T T</text><text x='180' y='70' class='sub ok'>T</text><text x='420' y='70' class='sub ok'>T</text><text x='20' y='105' class='sub ink'>T F</text><text x='180' y='105' class='sub no'>F</text><text x='420' y='105' class='sub ok'>T</text><text x='20' y='140' class='sub ink'>F T</text><text x='180' y='140' class='sub ok'>T</text><text x='420' y='140' class='sub ok'>T</text><text x='20' y='175' class='sub ink'>F F</text><text x='180' y='175' class='sub ok'>T</text><text x='420' y='175' class='sub no'>F</text><text x='20' y='225' class='tag ink'>A=B=C identical; D disagrees on rows (T,F) and (F,F)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Truth tables show P->Q, not Q->not P and not P or Q have the identical column, while not Q->P (which equals P or Q) differs on the rows P=T,Q=F and P=F,Q=F.

given.aim: Group the four formulas by their truth tables and report the equivalent set.

terms.meaning: the implication; false only when P is True and Q is False
terms.example: P=T, Q=F gives False
terms.connects: serves as the reference formula

terms.meaning: the contrapositive of P -> Q
terms.example: P=T, Q=F gives False (matches A)
terms.connects: equal to A by the contrapositive law

terms.meaning: the material-implication form of P -> Q
terms.example: P=T, Q=F gives F or F = False (matches A)
terms.connects: equal to A by material implication

terms.meaning: equals Q or P; true except when P and Q are both False
terms.example: P=F, Q=F gives False, but A gives True there
terms.connects: differs from A, so D is the odd one out

given.plan: Convert each option using the contrapositive and material-implication laws, then compare against the canonical form not P or Q.

steps.title: Rewrite A using material implication
steps.formula_raw: A -> B == not A or B
steps.apply: P -> Q == not P or Q, which is exactly option C. So A == C.
steps.note: C is just the standard or/not form of the same implication.

steps.title: Rewrite B (contrapositive) and confirm A == B
steps.formula_raw: (A -> B) == (not B -> not A)
steps.apply: not Q -> not P is the contrapositive of P -> Q, hence not Q -> not P == P -> Q. So B == A. Combined with Step 1, A == B == C.
steps.note: All three formulas have the column F only on the row P=T, Q=F.

steps.title: Check D against the group
steps.formula_raw: not Q -> P == not(not Q) or P == Q or P
steps.apply: not Q -> P simplifies to Q or P, which is False only when P=F and Q=F. There A=P->Q is True, so D differs from A on that row. D is not equivalent.
steps.note: Beware: an implication and its converse/inverse are generally not equivalent.

solution.result: A, B and C are logically equivalent (all equal P -> Q); D differs, so the equivalent set is A, B, C.

questions.formula_note: The contrapositive law and the material-implication law are the keys: they show P -> Q, not Q -> not P and not P or Q are one and the same truth function.

questions.question: Indicate which of the following well-formed formulae are valid: A. (P => Q) and (Q => R) => (P => R)   B. (P => Q) => (not P => not Q)   C. (P and (not P or not Q)) => Q   D. (P => R) or (Q => R) => ((P or Q) => R)
questions.to_find: Which formulas are valid (tautologies, true under every assignment).

understand.plain: A formula is valid when it is True for every assignment of P, Q, R. Option A is the famous hypothetical-syllogism law (chaining implications) and is always True. The other three each have at least one assignment making them False: B confuses contrapositive with inverse, C reduces to (P and not Q) => Q which fails when P is True and Q is False, and D fails when only one of P, Q forces R but the other does not.

keywords.explain: True under every truth assignment; the whole truth-table column is True
keywords.example: P or not P is valid

keywords.explain: from P=>Q and Q=>R you may conclude P=>R; the matching implication is a tautology
keywords.example: if 'rain=>wet' and 'wet=>cold' then 'rain=>cold'

keywords.explain: a single row where the formula is False, which is enough to break validity
keywords.example: B is broken by P=F, Q=T

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Validity check of four implications'><title>Which formulae are valid</title><desc>Only option A is a tautology; B, C and D each have a falsifying assignment.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='30' y='40' width='110' height='150' rx='12' class='hi'/><text x='85' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><text x='85' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='big ok'>valid</text><text x='85' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>syllogism</text><rect x='160' y='40' width='110' height='150' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='215' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B</text><text x='215' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='big no'>F</text><text x='215' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P=F,Q=T</text><rect x='290' y='40' width='110' height='150' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='345' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C</text><text x='345' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='big no'>F</text><text x='345' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P=T,Q=F</text><rect x='420' y='40' width='110' height='150' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='475' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>D</text><text x='475' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='big no'>F</text><text x='475' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>see step 4</text><text x='280' y='225' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>only A is true in every row</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Only option A (hypothetical syllogism) is a tautology; B fails at P=F,Q=T, C fails at P=T,Q=F, and D fails when exactly one of P,Q implies R.

given.aim: Decide validity of each implication and list the valid ones.

terms.meaning: propositional variables, each True or False
terms.example: P=T, Q=F, R=T is one of 8 assignments
terms.connects: validity must hold across all 2^3 = 8 rows

terms.meaning: X => Y is False only when X is True and Y is False
terms.example: T => F = False
terms.connects: the outer => of each option decides the whole value

terms.meaning: one assignment making the formula False, disproving validity
terms.example: for C take P=T, Q=F
terms.connects: finding one is enough to reject an option

given.plan: Recognise A as a known tautology; for B, C, D search for a single falsifying assignment, which immediately rules each out.

steps.title: A is the hypothetical-syllogism tautology
steps.apply: Suppose the antecedent (P=>Q) and (Q=>R) holds and P is True. Then Q is True (from P=>Q), then R is True (from Q=>R), so P=>R holds. The implication can never have a True antecedent with a False consequent, so A is valid.
steps.note: This chaining of implications is a standard tautology.

steps.title: B confuses contrapositive with inverse
steps.formula_raw: (P => Q) => (not P => not Q)
steps.apply: Take P=False, Q=True. Then P=>Q is True, but not P => not Q is True => False = False. So the whole formula is True => False = False. B is not valid.
steps.note: not P => not Q is the inverse of P=>Q, not its contrapositive.

steps.title: C simplifies and fails
steps.formula_raw: (P and (not P or not Q)) => Q == (P and not Q) => Q
steps.apply: Since P and not P = False, P and (not P or not Q) = P and not Q. Take P=True, Q=False: antecedent P and not Q = True, consequent Q = False, so the implication is True => False = False. C is not valid.
steps.note: Distributing P over the or kills the not P term.

steps.title: D fails with a single-implier assignment
steps.apply: Take P=True, Q=False, R=False. Then P=>R = False but Q=>R = True, so the antecedent (P=>R) or (Q=>R) = True. The consequent (P or Q)=>R = True => False = False. So D = True => False = False. D is not valid.
steps.note: or in the antecedent is too weak to force (P or Q) => R.

solution.result: Only A is valid; B, C and D each have a falsifying assignment, so the answer is A.

questions.formula_note: Hypothetical syllogism makes A a tautology; the trap is that the converse/inverse rewrites in B, C, D look plausible but each admits a counterexample row.

questions.question: If F_1, F_2 and F_3 are propositional formulae such that F_1 and F_2 -> F_3 and F_1 and F_2 -> not F_3 are both tautologies, then which of the following is true: A. Both F_1 and F_2 are tautologies   B. The conjunction F_1 and F_2 is not satisfiable   C. Neither is tautologous   D. Neither is satisfiable   E. None of the above
questions.answer: B. The conjunction F_1 and F_2 is not satisfiable
questions.to_find: What must follow when (F1 and F2) -> F3 and (F1 and F2) -> not F3 are both tautologies.

understand.plain: Both implications are tautologies, meaning that whenever F1 and F2 is True the conclusion must be True. But the two conclusions are F3 and not F3, which can never both be True at once. The only way out is that F1 and F2 is never True at all. Hence the conjunction F1 and F2 is unsatisfiable (not satisfiable).

keywords.explain: X -> Y is a tautology means: whenever X is True, Y is forced True
keywords.example: (p and not p) -> q is a tautology because the antecedent is never True

keywords.explain: True for at least one assignment; not satisfiable means False for every assignment
keywords.example: p and not p is not satisfiable

keywords.explain: a proposition and its negation cannot both be True simultaneously
keywords.example: you cannot have F3 = True and not F3 = True together

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Why F1 and F2 must be unsatisfiable'><title>F1 and F2 forced unsatisfiable</title><desc>If F1 and F2 were True it would force both F3 and not F3 True, a contradiction, so F1 and F2 must be False everywhere.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='30' y='80' width='150' height='70' rx='12' class='hi'/><text x='105' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>F1 and F2 = T ?</text><line x1='180' y1='115' x2='250' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='180' y1='115' x2='250' y2='150' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><rect x='250' y='50' width='130' height='55' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='315' y='84' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>F3 = T</text><rect x='250' y='130' width='130' height='55' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='315' y='164' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>not F3 = T</text><rect x='410' y='90' width='120' height='55' rx='10' class='no'/><text x='470' y='124' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>impossible</text><text x='280' y='218' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>so F1 and F2 is never True => not satisfiable</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Assuming F1 and F2 is True forces both F3 and not F3 to be True, which is impossible, so F1 and F2 must be unsatisfiable.

given.aim: Determine the forced property of F1 and F2 from the two tautology assumptions.

terms.meaning: arbitrary propositional formulae
terms.example: could be over any variables; only their joint behaviour matters
terms.connects: the constraints relate F1 and F2 to F3

terms.meaning: a tautology: whenever F1 and F2 is True, F3 is True
terms.example: if F1 and F2 holds then F3 holds
terms.connects: forces F3 True on every model of F1 and F2

terms.meaning: a tautology: whenever F1 and F2 is True, not F3 is True
terms.example: if F1 and F2 holds then F3 is False
terms.connects: forces F3 False on every model of F1 and F2

given.plan: Assume F1 and F2 has a satisfying assignment, derive both F3 and not F3 True on it (a contradiction), and conclude no such assignment exists.

steps.title: Suppose F1 and F2 is satisfiable
steps.formula_raw: If (X -> Y) is a tautology and X is True under I, then Y is True under I
steps.apply: Let I be an assignment making F1 and F2 True. Since (F1 and F2) -> F3 is a tautology, F3 is True under I. Since (F1 and F2) -> not F3 is also a tautology, not F3 is True under I.
steps.note: A True antecedent in a tautological implication forces the consequent.

steps.title: Reach a contradiction and conclude
steps.formula_raw: F3 = True and not F3 = True is impossible
steps.apply: Under I we would have both F3 = True and not F3 = True, which can never happen. So no assignment I makes F1 and F2 True; the conjunction F1 and F2 is not satisfiable.
steps.note: Note A, C, D do not follow in general - only B is forced.

solution.result: F1 and F2 must be unsatisfiable, so the correct option is B.

questions.formula_note: The crux: a tautological implication forces its consequent whenever the antecedent is True; forcing both F3 and not F3 is impossible, so the antecedent F1 and F2 can never be True (unsatisfiable).

questions.question: Which of the following is/are a tautology? A. a or b -> b and c   B. a and b -> b or c   C. a or b -> (b -> c)   D. a -> b -> (b -> c)
questions.answer: B. a and b -> b or c
questions.to_find: Which of the four formulas are tautologies (always True).

understand.plain: Read each formula with the usual precedence: not, then and/or, then ->. A tautology stays True for every choice of a, b, c. In B, a and b -> b or c: if the antecedent a and b is True then b is True, so b or c is True; the implication can never fail, so B is a tautology. The other three can be made False by a suitable assignment.

keywords.explain: not binds tightest, then and / or, then implication ->; this fixes how to parse each option
keywords.example: a or b -> b and c parses as (a or b) -> (b and c)

keywords.explain: a formula True for every assignment of its variables
keywords.example: a and b -> b is always True

keywords.explain: one row that makes an implication's antecedent True and consequent False
keywords.example: for A take a=T, b=F, c=F

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Tautology check of four formulas'><title>Which is a tautology</title><desc>Only B is a tautology; A, C and D have falsifying assignments.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='30' y='40' width='110' height='150' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='85' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><text x='85' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='big no'>F</text><text x='85' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a=T,b=F</text><rect x='160' y='40' width='110' height='150' rx='12' class='hi'/><text x='215' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B</text><text x='215' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='big ok'>taut</text><text x='215' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>always T</text><rect x='290' y='40' width='110' height='150' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='345' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C</text><text x='345' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='big no'>F</text><text x='345' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a=F,b=T,c=F</text><rect x='420' y='40' width='110' height='150' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='475' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>D</text><text x='475' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='big no'>F</text><text x='475' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>b=T,c=F</text><text x='280' y='222' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>only B is True in every row</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Only option B (a and b -> b or c) is a tautology; A, C and D each have an assignment that makes them False.

given.aim: Identify which formulas are True under every assignment of a, b, c.

terms.meaning: propositional variables, each True or False
terms.example: a=T, b=F, c=F is one of 8 rows
terms.connects: a tautology must survive all 8 rows

terms.meaning: conjunction is True only if both parts are True; disjunction is True if either is
terms.example: a and b with a=T, b=F is False
terms.connects: shapes the antecedent and consequent of each implication

terms.meaning: False only when its antecedent is True and consequent False
terms.example: T -> F = False
terms.connects: the outer -> determines the formula's truth value

given.plan: Test B by showing its antecedent forces its consequent; for A, C, D exhibit one assignment that makes the antecedent True and the consequent False.

steps.title: B is a tautology
steps.formula_raw: (a and b) -> (b or c)
steps.apply: If a and b is True then b is True, hence b or c is True. The implication never has a True antecedent with a False consequent, so B is always True - a tautology.
steps.note: Whenever both a and b hold, b alone already makes b or c True.

steps.title: A is falsifiable
steps.formula_raw: (a or b) -> (b and c)
steps.apply: Take a=True, b=False, c=False. Then a or b = True but b and c = False, giving True -> False = False. So A is not a tautology.
steps.note: The consequent requires both b and c, which is easy to break.

steps.title: C is falsifiable
steps.formula_raw: (a or b) -> (b -> c)
steps.apply: Take a=False, b=True, c=False. Then a or b = True and b -> c = True -> False = False, so the whole formula is True -> False = False. C is not a tautology.
steps.note: b True with c False breaks the inner implication.

steps.title: D is falsifiable
steps.apply: With right-association, take a=True, b=True, c=False. Then b -> c = False, so b -> (b -> c) = True -> False = False, and a -> False = True -> False = False. D is not a tautology.
steps.note: Whatever the grouping, b True and c False forces a False inner implication.

solution.result: Only B (a and b -> b or c) is a tautology, so the answer is B.

questions.formula_note: The principle (a and b) -> (b or c) is a tautology because b appears in both antecedent and consequent; A, C, D each break because their consequents demand more than the antecedent guarantees.

questions.question: Use Modus ponens (A, A -> B |= B) or resolution to show that the following set is inconsistent: 1. Q(x) -> P(x) or not R(a)   2. R(a) or not Q(a)   3. Q(a)   4. not P(a)   where x and y are universally quantified variables, a is a constant and P, Q, R are monadic predicates.
questions.answer: The set is inconsistent: instantiating clause 1 at x = a and resolving with Q(a), not P(a), R(a) derives the empty clause, so no model satisfies all four statements.
questions.to_find: A derivation (by resolution) of a contradiction showing the four statements cannot all be true.

understand.plain: Inconsistent means there is no assignment of truth values to the ground atoms P(a), Q(a), R(a) that makes all four statements true. We turn each statement into a clause, instantiate the universally quantified clause 1 at x = a, and then repeatedly resolve. The resolution chain collapses to the empty clause (a contradiction), which proves inconsistency.

keywords.explain: from clauses (X or L) and (Y or not L) infer the resolvent (X or Y); deriving the empty clause means contradiction
keywords.example: (P or R) and (not P) resolve to R

keywords.explain: a for-all x clause may be specialised by replacing x with any constant such as a
keywords.example: for-all x: Q(x) -> P(x) becomes Q(a) -> P(a)

keywords.explain: the resolvent of L and not L; it is always False and signals inconsistency
keywords.example: R(a) and not R(a) resolve to the empty clause

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 580 270' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Resolution refutation tree'><title>Resolution to the empty clause</title><desc>Clause 1 instantiated at a is resolved with Q(a), not P(a) and R(a) to derive the empty clause.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='262' y='42' class='sub ink'>Q(a)</text><rect x='120' y='80' width='180' height='34' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='102' class='sub ink'>P(a) or not R(a)</text><rect x='330' y='80' width='110' height='34' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='342' y='102' class='sub ink'>not P(a)</text><rect x='180' y='140' width='130' height='34' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='192' y='162' class='sub ink'>not R(a)</text><rect x='350' y='140' width='180' height='34' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='360' y='162' class='sub ink'>R(a) or not Q(a), Q(a)</text><rect x='360' y='195' width='90' height='34' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='372' y='217' class='sub ink'>R(a)</text><rect x='235' y='235' width='120' height='34' rx='8' class='no'/><text x='248' y='257' class='sub ink'>empty clause</text><line x1='120' y1='54' x2='200' y2='80' class='edge' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='290' y1='54' x2='220' y2='80' class='edge' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='210' y1='114' x2='245' y2='140' class='edge' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='385' y1='114' x2='275' y2='140' class='edge' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='245' y1='174' x2='295' y2='235' class='edge' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='405' y1='229' x2='305' y2='235' class='edge' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A resolution tree: instantiated clause 1 resolves with Q(a) to P(a) or not R(a), then with not P(a) to not R(a); clause 2 with Q(a) gives R(a); R(a) and not R(a) resolve to the empty clause.

given.aim: Derive a contradiction (empty clause) from the four statements, proving inconsistency.

terms.term: Clause 1: not Q(x) or P(x) or not R(a)
terms.meaning: the clause form of Q(x) -> P(x) or not R(a), universally quantified in x
terms.example: at x = a: not Q(a) or P(a) or not R(a)
terms.connects: instantiated at a to interact with the ground facts

terms.meaning: a disjunctive ground clause
terms.example: true if R(a) holds or Q(a) is false
terms.connects: combines with Q(a) to yield R(a)

terms.meaning: a positive ground unit fact
terms.example: Q(a) is asserted True
terms.connects: resolves against not Q(a) literals

terms.meaning: a negative ground unit fact
terms.example: P(a) is asserted False
terms.connects: resolves against the P(a) literal from clause 1

given.plan: Write each statement as a clause, universally instantiate clause 1 at x = a, then apply resolution step by step until the empty clause appears.

steps.title: Convert to clauses and instantiate
steps.formula_raw: for-all x F(x) |= F(a)
steps.apply: Clause 1 Q(x) -> P(x) or not R(a) becomes (not Q(x)) or P(x) or not R(a). Instantiate x = a to get C1a: not Q(a) or P(a) or not R(a). The other clauses are C2: R(a) or not Q(a), C3: Q(a), C4: not P(a).
steps.note: Material implication turns the arrow into a disjunction with the antecedent negated.

steps.title: Resolve C1a with Q(a) then not P(a)
steps.formula_raw: (X or L) , (Y or not L) |= (X or Y)
steps.apply: Resolve C1a with C3 (Q(a)) on the literal Q(a): get P(a) or not R(a). Resolve this with C4 (not P(a)) on P(a): get not R(a).
steps.note: Each unit fact cancels one literal of the instantiated clause.

steps.title: Derive R(a) and close with the empty clause
steps.formula_raw: (R(a) or not Q(a)) with Q(a) |= R(a);  R(a) with not R(a) |= empty clause
steps.apply: Resolve C2 (R(a) or not Q(a)) with C3 (Q(a)) to get R(a). Now resolve R(a) with the not R(a) from Step 2 to obtain the empty clause. A derivation of the empty clause means the clause set is unsatisfiable.
steps.note: The empty clause is the signature of an inconsistent set.

solution.result: Resolution derives the empty clause, so statements 1-4 cannot all hold simultaneously: the set is inconsistent.

questions.formula_note: Resolution refutation is the key tool: instantiating clause 1 at a and resolving against the ground units yields the empty clause, proving inconsistency. The official key marks this as N/A (proof/descriptive).

questions.question: Show that proposition C is a logical consequence of the formula A and (A -> (B or C)) and (B -> not A) using truth tables.
questions.answer: C is a logical consequence: in every interpretation that makes A and (A -> (B or C)) and (B -> not A) True, C is also True.
questions.to_find: Prove that whenever the given formula is True, C must be True.

understand.plain: C is a logical consequence of the formula means: every assignment that makes the whole formula True also makes C True. Build (or reason through) the truth table. The formula forces A True. Because A is True, B -> not A forces B to be False. Then A -> (B or C) with A True needs B or C True; since B is False, C must be True. So on every row where the formula is True, C is True as well.

keywords.explain: G is a consequence of F if every model of F is a model of G (F |= G)
keywords.example: p and q |= p

keywords.explain: an assignment that makes a formula True; only these rows matter for consequence
keywords.example: the single row A=T, B=F, C=T here

keywords.explain: a conjunct that must be True pins down its variables, e.g. A True forces B False via B -> not A
keywords.example: B -> not A with A True gives B = False

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Truth table forcing C true'><title>Why C follows from the formula</title><desc>The conjunction forces A true, then B false, then C true; the only satisfying row is A=T,B=F,C=T.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='30' y='40' width='150' height='60' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='105' y='66' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A = True</text><text x='105' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(conjunct A)</text><rect x='205' y='40' width='150' height='60' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='280' y='66' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B = False</text><text x='280' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>B -> not A</text><rect x='380' y='40' width='150' height='60' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='455' y='66' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>C = True</text><text x='455' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>A->(B or C)</text><line x1='180' y1='70' x2='205' y2='70' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='355' y1='70' x2='380' y2='70' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='280' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>only model: A=T, B=F, C=T</text><text x='280' y='200' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>formula True => C True, so formula |= C</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The conjunction forces A True, then B -> not A forces B False, then A -> (B or C) forces C True; the unique satisfying assignment is A=T, B=F, C=T, so C follows.

given.aim: Show that the formula logically entails C (every model of the formula has C True).

terms.meaning: asserted True by the conjunction
terms.example: for the formula to be True, A must be True
terms.connects: drives the values of B and C

terms.meaning: with A True, requires B or C to be True
terms.example: A True forces B or C = True
terms.connects: combined with B False, forces C True

terms.meaning: with A True (so not A False), forces B to be False
terms.example: if B were True then not A would be True, contradicting A True
terms.connects: pins B to False

terms.meaning: the proposition we want to show always follows
terms.example: we conclude C = True on every model
terms.connects: the logical consequence to prove

given.plan: Restrict to the rows where the whole conjunction is True; force A True, deduce B False from B -> not A, then deduce C True from A -> (B or C).

steps.title: The conjunction forces A True and B False
steps.formula_raw: From A True and (B -> not A): not A is False, so B must be False
steps.apply: For the formula F = A and (A -> (B or C)) and (B -> not A) to be True, every conjunct is True. So A = True. In B -> not A, not A = False; an implication with a False consequent forces a False antecedent, so B = False.
steps.note: This is modus tollens applied to B -> not A using A True.

steps.title: A -> (B or C) then forces C True
steps.formula_raw: From A True and (A -> (B or C)): B or C True; with B False, C True
steps.apply: Since A = True and A -> (B or C) is True, the consequent B or C is True. As B = False, the disjunction reduces to C, so C = True. Thus the only model of F is A=True, B=False, C=True, and there C is True.
steps.note: Every satisfying row of F has C True, so F |= C.

solution.result: On every interpretation making the formula True we get C = True, so C is a logical consequence of A and (A -> (B or C)) and (B -> not A).

questions.formula_note: Modus tollens (forcing B False from A True) and disjunctive syllogism (forcing C from B or C with B False) drive the proof; the formula has a single model A=T,B=F,C=T where C holds. The official key marks this as N/A (proof).

formulas.name: Validity of a Well-Formed Formula
formulas.plain: F is valid (a tautology) iff value(F, I) = True for every interpretation I
formulas.whenToUse: When asked to define or test whether a propositional formula is valid / a tautology.

terms.means: an interpretation: a truth assignment to all variables of F
the truth value of F computed under I

formulas.trap: Validity needs True for EVERY interpretation; True for just one interpretation is only satisfiability.

formulas.name: Material Implication Law
formulas.plain: A -> B == not A or B
formulas.whenToUse: To rewrite an implication as a disjunction (or vice versa) when comparing or simplifying formulas.
formulas.trap: Negate only the antecedent: not A or B, not not(A) and B.

formulas.name: Contrapositive Law
formulas.plain: A -> B == not B -> not A
formulas.whenToUse: To replace an implication by its (equivalent) contrapositive, and to distinguish it from the inverse/converse.
formulas.trap: The contrapositive (not B -> not A) is equivalent, but the inverse (not A -> not B) and converse (B -> A) are NOT.

formulas.name: Hypothetical Syllogism
formulas.plain: from P=>Q and Q=>R conclude P=>R; the matching implication is a tautology
formulas.whenToUse: To chain two implications transitively, or to recognise the chaining implication as valid.
formulas.trap: The order matters: you may chain P->Q with Q->R, not P->Q with R->Q.

formulas.name: Tautological Implication Forces Its Consequent
formulas.latex: (\models X \to Y) \land (X \text{ True under } I) \implies (Y \text{ True under } I)
formulas.plain: If X -> Y is a tautology and X is True under I, then Y is True under I
formulas.whenToUse: When a problem states an implication is a tautology and you need to propagate the antecedent being True.

terms.means: an interpretation under which X is True

formulas.trap: This forces Y only on assignments where X is actually True; it says nothing where X is False.

formulas.name: Conjunction Implies Shared-Term Disjunction
formulas.plain: (a and b) -> (b or c) is a tautology
formulas.whenToUse: To recognise a tautology where a conjunct of the antecedent also appears in the consequent disjunction.

terms.means: shared proposition appearing in both sides

formulas.trap: It works because b is in both sides; (a or b) -> (b and c) is NOT a tautology.

formulas.name: Universal Instantiation
formulas.plain: from for-all x F(x) infer F(a) for any constant a
formulas.whenToUse: To specialise a universally quantified statement to a particular constant before resolution.

terms.means: an open formula with free variable x

formulas.trap: You may substitute any term for x, but be consistent across the whole clause.

formulas.name: Resolution Rule
formulas.plain: from (X or L) and (Y or not L) infer the resolvent (X or Y); the empty clause means inconsistency
formulas.whenToUse: To refute a set of clauses by cancelling complementary literals until the empty clause appears.

terms.means: the literal resolved upon (clashing pair L and not L)
remaining literals of the first clause
remaining literals of the second clause

formulas.trap: Resolve on exactly one complementary literal pair per step; deriving the empty clause proves unsatisfiability.

formulas.name: Modus Tollens
formulas.plain: from P -> Q and not Q conclude not P
formulas.whenToUse: To deny the antecedent when the consequent of an implication is known False.
formulas.trap: Requires the consequent to be False (not Q); knowing Q False, not P follows, not the reverse.

formulas.name: Disjunctive Syllogism
formulas.plain: from P or Q and not P conclude Q
formulas.whenToUse: To eliminate a false disjunct and conclude the remaining one.

terms.means: the disjunct known to be False
the remaining disjunct that must then be True

formulas.trap: You must know one disjunct is actually False; an or alone does not pick out the other.
